title: The Freak
content:
Just beyond All Alone, going back,
Is the humpy of Hatter Magee.
We had travelled all day on the track,
And he offered us mutton and tea.
Mack is rather reserved, but will speak
On one theme, and with eloquence too, 
Thatï¿½s his angular chestnut, The Freak.
Hereï¿½s a tale that he told through the week,
And I try to believe it is true:

ï¿½True, he ainï¿½t no account ez a nag,
Anï¿½ Iï¿½m not goinï¿½ to boast of his blood;
If I liked I could pitch you a mag
ï¿½Bout his sire, once a prince of the stud;
Give performances coloured and plain,
Anï¿½ a pedigree long ez my arm, 
Which is style, but Iï¿½m straight in the main,
So he ainï¿½t of the Wangdoodle strain,
Nor his dam wasnï¿½t Kate nor The Charm.

ï¿½Fiddle-headed anï¿½ spavined! Well, pï¿½raps.
Yes, his legs is all over the shop,
Anï¿½ his pacinï¿½s described by the chaps
Ez a sort of a wallaby hop.
He ainï¿½t good over sticks, anï¿½ a mile
In four-thirtyï¿½s his best up to date;
Anï¿½ heï¿½s jest pure Gehenna fer guile,
But I wouldnï¿½t sell out fer a pile,
ï¿½Cause Iï¿½m not goin dog on a mate.

ï¿½See, Iï¿½m here, and heï¿½s yonder, of course,
But I might ï¿½aï¿½ been crow-bait by now, 
Once my life seemed to hang on that horse,
Anï¿½ I didnï¿½t get left. That is how!
Theyï¿½ve bin tellinï¿½ you, Billy anï¿½ Spence?
Ah, theyï¿½re mighty smart men down the creek,
Anï¿½ they wonï¿½t allow horses has sense,
But jest guy it ez chance or pretence
When I tell what was done by The Freak.

ï¿½But Iï¿½m here, anï¿½ heï¿½s there-thatï¿½s enough!
We were out ï¿½mong the Misery Hills.
ï¿½Course you donï¿½t know the country. Itï¿½s rough;
Anï¿½ the man that it corners it kills.
I canï¿½t figure what happened us quite,
But we came in a heap, me anï¿½ him.
When I knew who I was it was night,
Anï¿½ my head anï¿½ my chest wasnï¿½t right,
Anï¿½ the bone poked right outer this limb.

ï¿½Fer a spell I felt horribly sick
While I held there a meetinï¿½ of me;
Proposed, ï¿½It is U P with Dick,ï¿½
Put, anï¿½ carried unanermously.
Broken-legged, fifteen mile from the Creek, 
I weighed chances, anï¿½ gave up the case,
But I didnï¿½t deal fair by The Freak,
Till he limped to me, staggered anï¿½ weak,
Anï¿½ he flopped his ole lip in my face.

ï¿½Do? I fondled his nose like a fool,
Anï¿½ I called him love names without end;
Though I ainï¿½t a soft man as a rule,
There is times when I sorter unbend.
ï¿½Taint no use now to talk of the pain,
I endoored ez I struggled to climb
To his back from a log, or explain
How I fell back again anï¿½ again;
But I gave up exhausted in time,

ï¿½Anï¿½ I flung myself down on the ground,
Anï¿½ I cursed anï¿½, yes, maybe I cried,
But The Freak he came nosinï¿½ around,
Anï¿½ he rolled over right by my side.
Donï¿½t you try to explain, Iï¿½m content
That he knew jest ez well ez could be,
ï¿½Cos I looked in his eyes ez he bent,
By the Lord, anï¿½ I saw what he meant,
Anï¿½ thatï¿½s good enough talkinï¿½ fer me.

ï¿½Well, I crawled on his back ez he lay,
Anï¿½ he heaved himself up again, so,
Anï¿½ then struck out fer home, anï¿½ till day
I hung on to him, how I donï¿½t know.
Not a thing do I mind after that
ï¿½Fore I came round all right at the whim,
Spread out on the bunk of Big Mat,
With a doc. on the job from The Flat,
Anï¿½ my leg fairly timbered and trim.

ï¿½Yes, Iï¿½ve heard all the mag of the men, 
That he wanted to roll or to die,
Anï¿½ itï¿½s true that heï¿½s kicked me since then,
Anï¿½ heï¿½s likewise uncommonly sly;
But Iï¿½m here. If they talk fer a week
That one fact isnï¿½t goinï¿½ to change,
Anï¿½ I owe it this day to The Freak
That a crow isnï¿½t clippinï¿½ his beak
On my rib-bones out back by the range.ï¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Edward Dyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Edward Dyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
